Quick reference for projects and research
explanation:
**Normal health values** come from large population studies (NHANES, WHO, CDC). They vary by age, gender, ethnicity, and region. These are guidelines; your personal medical history always takes precedence.

## How It's Calculated

Normal: 95-100%. Low: 90-94%. Critical: <90% seek urgent care.

This calculator applies the formula with the values you enter, checking typical ranges and alerting when values are outside normal parameters.

## Normal Reference Values

| Parameter | Normal Adult Range |
|---|---|
| Blood Pressure | <120/80 mmHg |
| Resting Heart Rate | 60-100 bpm |
| Body Temperature | 36.1-37.2°C |
| SpO2 | 95-100% |
| Fasting Blood Glucose | 70-100 mg/dL |
| HbA1c | <5.7% |
| Total Cholesterol | <200 mg/dL |
| LDL | <100 mg/dL |
| HDL | >40 (Men) / >50 (Women) |

## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference guide. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult with a qualified professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: What is a normal blood oxygen saturation (SpO2) level?
faq.a: Normal SpO2 is 95-100% for most healthy adults. Levels of 90-94% are considered low, and below 90% requires urgent medical attention.

faq.q: When should I check my blood oxygen saturation?
faq.a: Check SpO2 if you have respiratory symptoms, shortness of breath, chest pain, or if you have a chronic condition affecting oxygen levels.

faq.q: What causes low blood oxygen levels?
faq.a: Low SpO2 can result from lung disease, heart conditions, high altitude, infections, anemia, or other medical conditions. Consult a doctor if your levels are persistently low.

faq.q: Is this calculator accurate for medical diagnosis?
faq.a: This is a reference tool only. For medical diagnosis, always consult a healthcare professional. SpO2 results should be confirmed with clinical assessment.
Pulse oximeters measure oxygen saturation non-invasively using light sensors placed on your fingertip or earlobe. They estimate the percentage of oxygen-carrying hemoglobin in your blood.

faq.q: Does altitude affect blood oxygen levels?
faq.a: Yes, SpO2 decreases at higher altitudes due to lower oxygen availability in the air. This is normal and typically reverses when you return to lower elevations.
Yes. Some medications, particularly those affecting heart rate or breathing, can influence SpO2 levels. Always inform your doctor of medications you're taking.

faq.q: When should I seek medical attention for low oxygen?
faq.a: Seek urgent care if SpO2 is below 90%, or if you experience severe shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, or bluish skin tone, regardless of SpO2 reading.

faq.q: Are normal oxygen levels the same for everyone?
faq.a: No. Oxygen saturation varies by age, altitude, fitness level, and underlying health conditions. Athletes and people living at high altitude may have different normal ranges.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, does not require external data updates
